metadata.title: The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media

abstract: This article uses qualitative and quantitative methods to analyse the entire corpus of news articles published about North Korea by six major UK national newspapers from 2000 to 2020. It offers insights on how North Korea evolved from a relatively obscure subject to a mainstay in the world news section, particularly for the tabloids. Coverage grew exponentially, led by not only stories about nuclear development and missile programmes, but also by soft news items like football, and tourism. Notably, the tone of coverage shifted after Kim Jong-un came to power in 2011, leading to an explosion of stories focussed on the young leader and his family. Structural topic modelling reveals 12 key topic areas among elite and tabloid media. While elite media coverage spanned a wider range of subjects, tabloid media largely focussed on three topics: missiles, the Kim family, and football. Each theme was highly compatible with the traditions of tabloid coverage, supplying eye-catching images (missiles and Kim's eccentric appearance), a 'royal family' of sorts, and sports (North Koreans' feats during World Cup). Furthermore, qualitative analysis demonstrates that while tabloidization and sensationalistic reporting on the Kim family largely drove the increase in coverage, such stories also added colour and depth previously lacking in reportage on the enemy regime, thereby making the portrayal of North Korea more multidimensional.

paragraphs: of global international reporting (Archetti, 2013), and boasts a highly differentiated news ecosystem in terms of content, readership, and ideology (Sparks, 1999).

This article analyses the entirety of media coverage over the last two decades. We are interested in depicting a fuller picture of this reporting, incorporating both quantitative and qualitative dimensions. We look at both tabloid and elite media, and in doing so, consider how the structure of representation changed over a 20-year period. We will begin with a literature review of three areas: (a) media coverage of the enemy and the 'global other' in Anglo-American media, (b) how North Korea has been portrayed by global news media, and (c) tabloidization and sensationalism as a representational trope in news media. These sections will be followed by a discussion of the methods used in this research, and finally, the findings and implications for future research.

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
paragraphs: Spurred by events like 9/11, changes in global politics in recent years have led to urgent attention to how news media make sense of the enemy and the 'global other'. News media plays a crucial role in this process, as the public's understanding of war and terrorismand ultimately, the people who are deemed adversariesis strongly shaped by press coverage (Cottle, 2006). Admittedly, there are dangers in overemphasizing the role of media. In their classic work on international reporting, Galtung and Ruge (1965) found it was possible to predict the likelihood of foreign coverage based on 12 factors including status, proximity, and cultural distance. This results in attention being concentrated on a handful of 'elite' countries seen as more consequential, such as the United States and Western European countries. For the non-elite countries to receive coverage, there must be major news-worthy events such as scandals, mega accidents, riots, and military takeovers (Galtung and Vincent, 1992: 52). In a similar vein, Harcup and O'Neil (2001) analysed determinants of international coverage, finding enduring patterns of international coverage reflecting the national interest and diplomatic relations that also mirrors governmental and bilateral agendas. In the case of the United States, Nohrstedt and Ottosen (2008) showed that American media create a 'threat' narrative that stresses danger, focussing on worst-case scenarios.

Enmification serves political purposes, such as building national ideologies (Lynch, 2015) and protecting the ego of group members (Oppenheimer, 2006). Although focussing on enemies and the dangers they pose to one's society may provide a heuristic shortcut for journalists and audiences alike, enemy images often contain biases and prejudices, presenting obstacles to analytical journalism (Ottosen, 1995). But enemy images themselves are not static, meaning they too are subject to changes. Lowenstein-Barkai (2021) found that positive change in media representation of enemy leaders can be conducive to a more reconciliatory, positive social climate.

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
sections.header_raw: How global media cover North Korea
sections.header_norm: how global media cover north korea
paragraphs: Media coverage of North Korea retains attributes of Cold War-era reporting, including Us and Them binaries and a hawkish focus on military strategies. Comparing media coverage in six countries, Seo (2009) finds that coverage was largely driven by national interest. Anglo-American media often failed to explain contextual factors that led to the nuclear crisis and the culpability of the West when it comes to tensions in East Asia (Dalton et al., 2016). The US coverage tends to favour moral binaries, assigning all blame for conflict in the region on North Korea and/or Kim Jong-un (Curran and Gibson, 2019).

Japanese media tends to be hawkish (Lynn, 2006), while coverage by Chinese and Russian media sympathetically emphasizes cooperation over confrontation, again reflecting the positions of their respective governments (Chung et al., 2014). Similarly, Dai and Hyun (2010) find a common threat frame in coverage of NK by South Korean and Chinese media, although specific frames vary slightly according to national interests and strategies. In their analysis of New York Times coverage, Lim and Seo (2009) identify three competing frames: threat, human rights, and dialogue partner. A shift toward the dialogue partner frame was followed by changes in public opinions choosing economic sanctions over military solutions. Similarly, Hollihan et al. (2021) find an interplay between domestic politics and international relations in critical junctures like the US-North Korea and Inter-Korean summit meetings.

In recent years, though, researchers have detected changing patterns in the global media's treatment of North Korea, including an increasing focus on soft news. Seo (2018) finds that such coverage is more common than previously realized, even in outlets that do not share the Associated Press' physical presence in Pyongyang. Within journalism studies, there is an increasing realization of the need to pay attention to a wider variety of stories including travel, food, music, and fashion, away from traditional journalism's privileging of the political realm (Zelizer, 2011). Such 'lifestyle journalism' is crucial in producing an expanded picture of the world (Hanusch, 2013).

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
sections.header_raw: UK media context: The elite versus tabloid press and emotional turn
sections.header_norm: uk media context the elite versus tabloid press and emotional turn
paragraphs: Scholars have documented an overall tabloidization of the press, noting changes in the topics being covered (more entertainment, less information), and in forms of presentation (more pictures) (Esser, 1999). Vligenthart et al. (2011) found this tendency to be particularly apparent in political news in the United Kingdom and Germany, with coverage becoming more personalised, negative, and conflict-driven in recent years. With his comparative analysis of UK and German media, Esser (1999) asserts that tabloidization is determined by three factors: journalistic values, media cultures and economics, and legal conditions.

In addition to the industry-wide tabloidization of media, UK media has retained a strong tabloid press tradition at the newsstands. Unlike in the United States, where commercial radio became the preferred medium for mass audiences (Picard, 1998), tabloids dominated the circulation figures in the United Kingdom. The Sun has one of the highest circulations in the world, meaning that any study that omits the tabloid press will provide very limited analysis of the British media (Sparks, 2010). In the United Kingdom, the media is also more heterogeneous in terms of ideology than in most other Western countries, a reality that extends to the tabloids: While most tabloid papers lean conservative, the labour-affiliated tabloid Daily Mirror remains popular.

Another way for scholars to describe tabloidization is to equate it with a decline in journalistic standards and in hard news like political reporting. However, researchers have also begun to note changes coming from elite media, which is becoming softer in subject and style. For example, Wahl-Jorgenson (2020) writes of an 'emotional turn' in journalism, referring to the rise of mediated emotion different from individual emotion. Research shows the prevalence of story personification, such as a focus on a single victim of a given geopolitical event, often results in a strong emotional response from readers and increased support for intervention (Maier et al., 2017). In foreign news, personification often weakens analytical perspectives and the source-critical attitude necessary for coverage that is contextual and in-depth (Ottosen, 1995). While story personification in news coverage is often considered bad journalism, it can help the audience recall the news better (Mujica and Bachmann, 2018).

Our research on how both tabloids and elite outlets cover an unfamiliar subject like North Korea is thus ideally situated to deepen our understanding of the contemporary news environment. The biggest research questions can be summarized as follows:

RQ1: What are the dominant themes in coverage of North Korea in UK media and how have these themes evolved? RQ2: How does North Korea's image as a threat and foe get constructed? RQ3: What are the differences in North Korea coverage by tabloids and broadsheet newspapers?

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
paragraphs: This research combines quantitative and qualitative analysis of UK media coverage of North Korea. We combined large-scale computerized analysis with critical discourse analysis of six national newspapers: The Sun, Daily Mail, Daily Mirror, The Times, Financial Times and the Guardian, over two decades (See Table 1 for an overview of publications and number of qualifying articles). We chose these six outlets based on the circulation figures, their representation of elite and tabloid newspapers groups, and political diversity. The chosen papers also reflect the highly differentiated nature of UK media, with political stances ranging from pro-Labour to pro-economic liberalism. This broad inclusion provides the opportunity to explore varying discourses and reporting styles and reflect public opinions from audiences of diverse backgrounds. We used the search string 'North Korea' for articles published from January 2000 to December 2020. We used two databases. The articles from five media outlets (The Sun, Daily Mail, Daily Mirror, The Times, Financial Times) were downloaded from ProQuest. The LexisNexis database was used for The Guardian as the ProQuest database was found to have gaps in database, yielding far fewer (4094 articles) compared to LexisNexis (8448) for the same query. This process led to an initial sample size of 43,810 articles.

To come up with a robust dataset three researchers read and filtered the entirety of articles to only select articles dealing with North Korea. To make the cut, North Korea or its inhabitants had to be featured prominently. Articles in which North Korea appeared briefly or tangentially were not included. For example, numerous articles merely mentioning North Korea as one of the 'Axis of Evil' countries alongside Iran and Iraq were eliminated, unless there was significant mention of North Korea's military programmes or diplomatic repercussions. Articles shorter than 100 words were also eliminated. News summaries, editorials, reviews, and letters to editors were also removed. For duplicate articles or multiple versions, only the last version was left for analysis. The process yielded 8144 articles for analysis. To test intercoder reliability in selecting the 8144 articles for analysis, we randomly chose 30 articles to be coded independently by the second coder. Krippendorff's alpha coefficient was 0.864, which was deemed sufficient (Krippendorff, 2004). Average pairwise percent agreement was 93.33%.

The LexisNexis database was used for The Guardian as the ProQuest database was found to have gaps in database, yielding far fewer (4094 articles) compared to LexisNexis (8448) for the same query. This process led to an initial sample size of 43,810 articles.

To come up with a robust dataset three researchers read and filtered the entirety of articles to only select articles dealing with North Korea. To make the cut, North Korea or its inhabitants had to be featured prominently. Articles in which North Korea appeared briefly or tangentially were not included. For example, numerous articles merely mentioning North Korea as one of the 'Axis of Evil' countries alongside Iran and Iraq were eliminated, unless there was significant mention of North Korea's military programmes or diplomatic repercussions. Articles shorter than 100 words were also eliminated. News summaries, editorials, reviews, and letters to editors were also removed. For duplicate articles or multiple versions, only the last version was left for analysis. The process yielded 8144 articles for analysis. sections.header_norm: quantitative text analysis structural topic modelling
paragraphs: The entire corpus of media coverage was analysed using topic modelling, a mode of computer-assisted textual analysis widely used for the analysis of large-scale textual data (Alix et al, 2015;Bail, 2012). Topic modelling assumes that there is a set of topics in a collection (the number of which is specified in advance). It works by producing a set of interpretable ''topics'' (i.e., groups of words associated with a single theme) and assessing the strength with which each document exhibits those topics. The main advantage of such topic modelling is its ability to capture the meanings that emerge out of relations rather than residing within words (DiMaggio et al., 2013).

We used structural topic modelling (STM) to understand the relationship between types of newspapers (tabloid and elite) and the topics, including changes over time. The model's key strength is that it permits users to incorporate metadata, defined as information about each document, into the topic model. (Roberts et al., 2019). We put two covariates, a dummy variable (tabloid and elite media) and publication date to see how the proportion of a topic changes over time.

To extract core information and delete noisy information for the statistical analyses, the following pre-processing operations were conducted: switching all characters to lowercase, removing punctuation, removing numbers, removing double spaces, removing stop words, and stemming. Stemming is the process to reduce reflected or derived words to their word stem. For example, words like 'launches', 'launcher' and 'launched' are transformed to 'launch'. Lastly, we dropped words that did not appear in more than four documents.

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
paragraphs: This article also uses critical discourse analysis, where discourse includes written and spoken forms as well as the social relations and power structures surrounding them. As Fairclough (1995) said, the aim of a discourse analysis is to understand the 'systematic links between texts, discourse practices, and sociocultural practices' (p. 17). Journalists work for organizations with specific discursive tendencies, with their language that represents 'opinion statements … embedded in argumentation that makes them more or less defensible, justifiable, or legitimate as conclusions' (Van Dijk, 1997, p. 24).

For us it was particularly important to see how the discourse of North Korea as an enemy gets constructed and perpetuated in the news media. To begin with, the authors first identified articles corresponding with the topics which emerged as more relevant via quantitative analysis. After that, the authors focussed on who the main figures were in the text; what discourses the stories drew from; and what social and political contexts could be inferred from the stories. The combined approach of quantitative and qualitative analysis expands the validity and reliability of the findings on how media coverage changed over time.

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
paragraphs: The volume of North Korea coverage jumped nearly ten times from 2000 to 2017, when coverage was at its highest, as Figure 1 illustrates. On average, the volume of North Korea-related coverage increased by 4-5 times from the early 2000s to 2020. Overall, there were five peaks of coverage, in years 2002-2003, 2006, 2009-2010, 2013 and 2017. The first two periods coincided largely with security and diplomatic tensions, as concerns over North Korea's weapons of mass destruction arose as the country was called part of the 'Axis of Evil' by George W. Bush and conducted the first nuclear test. The 2009-2010 period marked Kim Jong-il's death and the emergence of Kim Jong-un. In other words, coverage largely mirrored the political and diplomatic tensions surrounding North Korea. This was particularly true for the elite newspapers Guardian, Times, and Financial Times. Financial Times showed the paper's orientation towards markets and the economy, with a marked increase of coverage in the 2002-2003 period when North Korea experimented with reform with a flurry of economic as well as social and cultural exchanges. In comparison, the left-leaning Guardian showed less interest until 2010, a year when it dominated the headlines with soft and hard stories including not only the transition of power but also the FIFA World Cup, Wikileaks and the sinking of the South Korean warship Cheonan.

The year 2013, however, was a meaningful year that stood out for several reasons. First, the increase in coverage was equally strong across all UK outlets, elite and tabloid alike, unlike in previous waves when coverage patterns of the two categories differed. Coverage more than doubled in all outlets. Moreover, stories that year dealt with a set of extraordinary events happening in North Korea usually overlooked by international media: domestic politics, with the purge of major political figures in North Korea by Kim Jong-un. Also prominent were defector-driven stories of human rights violation in North Korea, as well as UK-North Korea bilateral stories such as a controversial visit of UK academics in Pyongyang.

The next peak in 2017 was far bigger than any previous peaks and was the result of an even more diverse set of events happening across the board. Major events happening in that year included, among others, summit meetings between Donald Trump and Kim Jong-un.

With STM topic modelling, we extracted 12 topics: nuclear programme (Topic #1), human rights (#2), inter-Korean relations (#3), Japan-North Korea relations (#4), US-North Korea relations (#5), missiles and rockets (#6), Kim Jong-un and family (#7), film (#8), tourism (#9), football and sports (#10), arrest and illegal acts by North Korea (#11), and the economy (#12). We then derived the words frequently associated with each topic group, which can be seen in Table 2.

When we plot which topics are more associated with which outlets with STM analysis, we get the results represented in Figure 2. Topics with positive values on the x-axis indicate a higher correlation with elite newspapers, and negative values indicate a higher association with the tabloids. The three elite newspapers had a clear preference for harder, policy-driven stories on economy (Topic 12), the nuclear programme (Topic 1), as well as bilateral relationships with South Korea, Japan and the United States (Topics 3, 4 and 5, respectively).

In contrast, the tabloids mostly preferred softer news stories on Kim Jong-un and his family (Topic 7), and tourism and trends in North Korea (Topic 9), film and cinema (Topic 8), as well as sports (Topic 10). Topic 6 (missile and rockets) is the lone exception.

Table 2. Frequently found works exclusively associated with each topic group. Figure 3(b) shows changes in the way the media cover North Korea's military programmes and how the markets responded to them. Stories about North Korea's nuclear programmesuch as stories on the Yongbyon reactors and extradition of weapons-grade plutonium from fuel rodsdecreased markedly after 2010. In contrast, reports on missiles and rocketswhich could potentially carry nuclear weaponsincreased. One possible way to interpret this is that the news media has come to accept North Korea's nuclear arsenal as a given and has decided to focus instead on newer developments such as missiles. Despite the fluctuation in the military and nuclear-related stories, economy-related storieswhich often deal with the reaction of the global stock market in the geopolitically sensitive Northeast Asiareacted with more calm, as the flatter curves indicate. Figure 3(d) shows a steady rise of stories about Kim Jong-un and his family, which peaked after Kim Jong-il's death in 2011. Stories about human rights violations and other criminal acts by North Korea also increased in response to events such as the arrests of foreigners and the assassination of Kim Jong-un's half-brother Kim Jong-nam.

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
paragraphs: Lowenstein-Barkai (2021) presented six different types of enmification used by mass media in times of conflict: stereotyping, demonization or dehumanization, moral differentiation, negative accusation, exaggeration and treating the enemy as a faceless mass. From Palestine to Northern Ireland, depiction of the enemy as evil and irrational has been observed. Representation of the enemy leader is particularly important as the person is seen as exemplary of the group's behaviour (Balmas, 2017). For example, in the run up to the Gulf War, Saddam Hussein had been depicted as an irrational person who personified evil, providing the West with the moral justification for the war (Rojo, 1995). Overall, news coverage of North Korea was highly negative, showing most of the six types mentioned above. Particularly prevalent were stories stereotyping the country as singularly irrational, crazy and weird. 'Eccentric' and synonyms like 'bizarre' and 'weird' were commonly used to describe the regime, or 'kooks with nukes' as a Daily Mirror headline called it (8 April 2013). Negativity spiked in 2013 and 2014 when Kim Jong-un executed several high-ranking figures, including his powerful uncle Jang Song-thaek.

Compared to the tabloids, coverage at the three elite outlets was predictably less negative and emotional, hewing close to the tradition of diplomatic and policy reporting. The Guardian, in particular, stood out for detailed coverage of the peace process and exchange programmes when North Korea experimented with reform in the early 2000s, including the restoration of London-Pyongyang diplomatic ties in 2000 when it pointedly wrote that a policy of containment will no longer work (21 October 2000). Among the tabloids, coverage at the Labour-leaning Daily Mirror was more dovish compared to others, for example, with stories calling for dialogue to defuse tensions and highlighting the plight of children due to food shortages (6 March 2003).

News stories not only demonized Pyongyang and depicted the Kim family as crazy, but also exaggerated to highlight the uncivilized nature of the regime. Tabloids like the Sun, Daily Mail and Daily Mirror frequently ran stories with racist undertones. One story claimed cannibalism is rampant in North Korea, with parents eating their children (Sun, 28 January 2013). Another said North Korea deliberately kills babies with disabilities, castrates those with dwarfism, and uses chemical weapons to decimate villages (Daily Mail, 12 December 2012). The barbaric images were reinforced with the mention of dog-eating practices, which is a culturally accepted practice throughout the Korean peninsula. Kim Jong-un was called a 'porky dog-munching dictator' (Sun, 15 September 2016). Reference to short stature is another frequently appearing topic in descriptions of Kim Jong-un and Kim Jong-il. Even the Guardian was not free from such racist coverage ridiculing the shortness of Asians, reporting Kim Jong-il's height of 162 cm in a story titled 'The question: Who is the shortest world leader?' (4 March 2008)

Tabloids like the Sun, Daily Mail and Daily Mirror frequently ran stories with racist undertones. One story claimed cannibalism is rampant in North Korea, with parents eating their children (Sun, 28 January 2013). Another said North Korea deliberately kills babies with disabilities, castrates those with dwarfism, and uses chemical weapons to decimate villages (Daily Mail, 12 December 2012). The barbaric images were reinforced with the mention of dog-eating practices, which is a culturally accepted practice throughout the Korean peninsula. Kim Jong-un was called a 'porky dog-munching dictator' (Sun, 15 September 2016). Reference to short stature is another frequently appearing topic in descriptions of Kim Jong-un and Kim Jong-il. (4 March 2008)

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
paragraphs: In one important aspect, coverage of North Korea differed from existing patterns of enmification by news media: reports did not blur the identity of the North Korean regime and reduce it to a faceless mass, as is often the case when journalists create enemy images and stereotypes in times of conflict (Musa and Ferguson, 2013). While some unidimensional stories were depicting North Koreans largely as a brainwashed mass incapable of dissent, such reports were drowned out as coverage of the country became increasingly diverse and colourful.

A prime example of this can be seen with personalization, especially how Kim Jong-un has been covered over the two decades. The tabloid newspapers exhaustively and incessantly covered Kim Jong-un over the years. Even considering the sensationalistic language the paper is known for, the Sun, for example, depicts Kim with particular spice and flair. Headlines of this newspaper had a field day using Kim's name in the headlines, with 'Kim Bomb Un' (10 September 2016), 'Slim Jong Un' (10 July 2016), 'Kim Jong Kill' (11 February 2016), and 'Air Force Un' (11 January 2016), 'Kim Jong One' (3 December 2015), and 'Trim Jong Un' (27 November 2015).

Over time, however, such ridicule congeals to a genuine interest in Kim and his family, and ultimately, the future of North Korea. After Kim Jong-il's death and the emergence of his son, British media increasingly referred to the next generation as a royal family, a familiar frame for the British news audience. In this story, Kim Jong-un's sister Yo-jong is the princess. Stories about the 'North Korean princess' went beyond the usual formula of looks and fashion to detail her potential role in the North Korean government, as this article shows:

Along with Kim's wife, the former singer Ri Sol-ju, the 27-year-old is one of the country's most prominent women and has been seen with her brother on public visits. However, her absence from the limelight adds weight to the theory that she may have taken over some, or even all, of the leadership responsibilities from her brother as he recovers from his 'discomfort'. (Guardian, 10 October 2014).

After Kim Jong-un executed his uncle, UK news outlets extended the royal analogy to unspool a Gothic tale of an insecure young king who had turned into a madman. But the heightened interest in North Korea also provided space for more complicated, nuanced, and unconventional stories featuring the Kim family. When Kim Jong-il died, the Guardian ran a story on how his death was being mourned by allies like Cuba, which declared three days of mourning, providing the context that Pyongyang had been one of the leading forces behind the creation of the non-aligned movement within the Communist Bloc.

Similarly, sophisticated stories appeared even in the tabloids, with the Sun running an article titled 'He's a bad guy, but no Bond villain', which contextualized the crisis: 'Kim is no fanatic … still young and green, he needs to prove his mettle to the tough generals around him'. The story mentioned the complicated geopolitics in Northeast Asia which makes a full-on war impossible as it would automatically implicate China and the United States (Sun, 30 August 2013).

A confluence of soft news stories, particularly in sports, also created a picture of North Korea other than as pure evil or a dangerous enemy. Soccerfootball in UK Englishis a case in point. UK's fascination with North Korean soccer has roots in the latter's participation in the 1966 England World Cup, where the impoverished country left a strong impression as underdogs who beat Italy to advance into the quarterfinals. The connection led to UK news stories on not only North Korea's performance in the contemporary soccer scene, but also human-interest stories on British fans of North Korean soccer, some of whom even made the trip to Pyongyang to meet with retired soccer players from 1966. As such, the soccer stories, while not being the most numerous in terms of numbers, contributed to portraying the enemy in a more humane light, as this Guardian article clearly shows: It's hard not to root for plucky North Korea, if only because you can't imagine any of the players demanding an air-clearing meeting with the manager after a lacklustre group-stage performance. They just get out there and give it everything they've got, until the blood runs from their socks, in accordance with the Juche Idea (22 June 2010). Such a depiction is exceptionally warm and welcoming, especially considering the continuing military standoff surrounding North Korea. More importantly, with these stories the North Korean athletes became three-dimensional beings with individual playing styles, as opposed to dog-eating automatons brainwashed by the regime. Pyongyang also recognized the opportunity to foster bilateral affinity with the UK via soccer: in December 2010, North Korean state TV aired Bend it like Beckham, a story of adolescent female soccer players in the United Kingdom. It was reportedly the first Western movie that was shown to the North Korean public in recent memory (Guardian, 31 December 2010).

British media continued to follow the sports thread with Dennis Rodman's visit to North Korea, and subsequently with North Korea's participation in Pyeongchang Winter Olympics, to which they sent Kim Jong-un's sister and high-profile musicians like Hyon Song-wol to South Korea.

Taken together, the changes in the quality and quantity of North-Korea related news show that British journalists have a bigger menu to choose from when it comes to North Korea. Unlike in the early 2000s when a handful of stories would detail military developments, UK media are comfortable covering a wide array of topics and themes, including music, theatre and architecture. For example, a Guardian story on Pyongyang's changing skyline explains Kim Jong-un's success in modernizing the housing stock in the capital city: Kim Jong-un's nuclear threats and ruthless executions dominate headlines abroad; yet in Pyongyang he is carving out a reputation as a champion of modern facilities for the country's new middle class (11 September 2015).

The changes in the image of the enemy -North Koreain UK media from 2000 to 2020 happened alongside a roller-coaster ride of political events surrounding the country, from talks and reform drive to nuclear and missile tests. While it is outside the scope of this paper to determine the effects of media, our findings lead us to question if news media may have contributed to alleviating tensions in UK-North Korea relations after a certain point by promoting a deeper interestand, ultimately, understanding of North Korea among the British public after the restoration of diplomatic ties.

sections.paper_id: Seo et al. - 2023 - The ‘Kim Jong-un Effect’ and the mainstreaming of North Korea coverage in UK Media
paragraphs: This study focussed on investigating the UK media's coverage of North Korea by the following coverage in six major newspapers over 20 years. The objective of this research was to identify key themes and topics and trace changes over time. We used a novel approach combining multiple computerized quantitative methods with painstaking qualitative analysis in multiple stages. By doing so, we hoped to understand how the image of North Korea was constructed and evolved.

Our most significant finding concerns the explosive growth of coverage over the 20 years. At the turn of the 20th century, North Korea had been one of the numerous foreign countries making sporadic appearances in the newspapers. When Kim Jong-il started experimenting with reform and opened North Korea's doors wide to outsiders in 2000 and 2001, UK newspapers largely ignored the developments. Fast forward to 2020, when North Korea is a mainstay for journalists, with stories on politics, culture, and sports, the kind of beat journalists can mine for soft stories when news is slow.

The 'mainstreaming' of North Korea as a journalistic subject has multiple layers. Overall, coverage shows excessive sensationalism and demonization, especially in the tabloids. While elite outlets are less sensational, they also use North Korea as entertainment by making fun of Kim Jong-un and the allegedly brainwashed people. The frequent personification of the enemy, particularly the media's obsession with the North Korean royal family, offered the UK audience an easy heuristic with which to understand the ruling power in North Korea. At the same time, however, it could also have contributed to North Korea's desire to put its leader in the centre of the limelight. According to North Korea's ruling Juche ideology, the leader is a central figure who commands god-like respect (Armstrong, 2005). The main TV news programme in North Korea often starts with the leader's itinerary for the day, putting it squarely in the centre. Thus it is also conceivable that Pyongyang officials may have considered it an achievement that their leader was being featured so prominently in global news media, however negative such depictions might be. The situation regarding North Korea parallels the Western media's coverage of other enemies like the Islamic State. Courty et al. (2019) showed the Western media unwittingly became complicit in spreading Islamic State propaganda with its sensationalistic and alarmist coverage.

Commercialism and economic success were major drivers of North Korea-related stories. The tabloid newspapers Sun, Daily Mirror and Daily Mail capitalized on the attention-grabbing nature of the subjectas well as the strong visuals North Korea presentedto churn stories on a country they previously wrote little about. The pro-business Financial Times was interested in opportunities for global businesses, as well as fluctuations in the global finance market, as their coverage patterns clearly mirror these developments. But perhaps tellingly, even the highbrow Guardian, around the year 2010, began to publish more detailed, specialized stories on North Korea, establishing a standalone corner titled 'North Korea Networks' with emphasis on soft news and lifestyle journalism like tourism in the so-called 'hermit regime'.

It is hard to deny that deprivations and human rights violations exist in a grand scale in North Korea. Those with strong views on the matter may find it problematic that North Korea is increasingly getting the soft-news treatment by news media when they can publish more stories of human rights violation. However, journalism scholars increasingly challenge the duality of popular versus quality, or political versus non-political, journalism (Meijer, 2001). Scholars like Wahl-Jorgensen (2020) believe journalism has entered a new era in which emotion occupies a much more important position than before. In this era, even hard news genres like political news or investigative stories cannot work without strong emotional storytelling, via routinized practices Wahl-Jorgensen calls 'strategic ritual emotionality'. Even in Pulitzer Prize-winning news stories, journalists routinely use personalized storytelling and human-interest stories without abandoning the needs of objective journalism. In the case of North Korea, the emotionality and personalized storytelling had the effect of adding colour and depth often lacking in coverage of an enemy regime, eventually paving the way for more nuanced, multidimensional coverage.

Researchers studying emotionality and everyday journalism thus call for more research on the role of emotions, from news epistemologies to emotions in text, production processes, and audience engagement (Hanusch, 2013;Wahl-Jorgensen, 2020). But there is a wide range of emotions, some of which may have the opposite effect on the audience. Story personification has been known to bolster support for military intervention (Maier et al., 2017), but it could also have the opposite effect of energizing peace and dialogue in Northern Ireland (Lowenstein-Barkai, 2021). In the case of North Korea, the latter resonates more. Future research could thus look more closely into different elements of personification and see how they contribute to different affective appeals.

In addition, more research is also needed to determine the production processes of journalism involving foreign countries like North Korea. The objective environment of international reporting has changed a lot over the last two decades with the shutdown of elite foreign bureaus, and insights from the actual fields of journalism (which may be newsrooms thousands of miles away from the place being covered) would help us understand the changes in representation. And lastly, comparative research of coverage involving North Korea, not only with other countries like the United States and Japan but also historically with other 'enemies' like the Soviet Union would provide insight on how the West makes sense of those deemed adversaries.

In the case of North Korea, the emotionality and personalized storytelling had the effect of adding colour and depth often lacking in coverage of an enemy regime, eventually paving the way for more nuanced, multidimensional coverage.

Researchers studying emotionality and everyday journalism thus call for more research on the role of emotions, from news epistemologies to emotions in text, production processes, and audience engagement (Hanusch, 2013;Wahl-Jorgensen, 2020). But there is a wide range of emotions, some of which may have the opposite effect on the audience. Story personification has been known to bolster support for military intervention (Maier et al., 2017), but it could also have the opposite effect of energizing peace and dialogue in Northern Ireland (Lowenstein-Barkai, 2021). In the case of North Korea, the latter resonates more. Future research could thus look more closely into different elements of personification and see how they contribute to different affective appeals.

In addition, more research is also needed to determine the production processes of journalism involving foreign countries like North Korea. The objective environment of international reporting has changed a lot over the last two decades with the shutdown of elite foreign bureaus, and insights from the actual fields of journalism (which may be newsrooms thousands of miles away from the place being covered) would help us understand the changes in representation. And lastly, comparative research of coverage involving North Korea, not only with other countries like the United States and Japan but also historically with other 'enemies' like the Soviet Union would provide insight on how the West makes sense of those deemed adversaries.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
This study focussed on investigating the UK media's coverage of North Korea by the following coverage in six major newspapers over 20 years. The objective of this research was to identify key themes and topics and trace changes over time. We used a novel approach combining multiple computerized quantitative methods with painstaking qualitative analysis in multiple stages. By doing so, we hoped to understand how the image of North Korea was constructed and evolved.

Our most significant finding concerns the explosive growth of coverage over the 20 years. At the turn of the 20th century, North Korea had been one of the numerous foreign countries making sporadic appearances in the newspapers. When Kim Jong-il started experimenting with reform and opened North Korea's doors wide to outsiders in 2000 and 2001, UK newspapers largely ignored the developments. Fast forward to 2020, when North Korea is a mainstay for journalists, with stories on politics, culture, and sports, the kind of beat journalists can mine for soft stories when news is slow.

The 'mainstreaming' of North Korea as a journalistic subject has multiple layers. Overall, coverage shows excessive sensationalism and demonization, especially in the tabloids. While elite outlets are less sensational, they also use North Korea as entertainment by making fun of Kim Jong-un and the allegedly brainwashed people. The frequent personification of the enemy, particularly the media's obsession with the North Korean royal family, offered the UK audience an easy heuristic with which to understand the ruling power in North Korea. At the same time, however, it could also have contributed to North Korea's desire to put its leader in the centre of the limelight. According to North Korea's ruling Juche ideology, the leader is a central figure who commands god-like respect (Armstrong, 2005). The main TV news programme in North Korea often starts with the leader's itinerary for the day, putting it squarely in the centre. Thus it is also conceivable that Pyongyang officials may have considered it an achievement that their leader was being featured so prominently in global news media, however negative such depictions might be. The situation regarding North Korea parallels the Western media's coverage of other enemies like the Islamic State. Courty et al. (2019) showed the Western media unwittingly became complicit in spreading Islamic State propaganda with its sensationalistic and alarmist coverage.

Commercialism and economic success were major drivers of North Korea-related stories. The tabloid newspapers Sun, Daily Mirror and Daily Mail capitalized on the attention-grabbing nature of the subjectas well as the strong visuals North Korea presentedto churn stories on a country they previously wrote little about. The pro-business Financial Times was interested in opportunities for global businesses, as well as fluctuations in the global finance market, as their coverage patterns clearly mirror these developments. But perhaps tellingly, even the highbrow Guardian, around the year 2010, began to publish more detailed, specialized stories on North Korea, establishing a standalone corner titled 'North Korea Networks' with emphasis on soft news and lifestyle journalism like tourism in the so-called 'hermit regime'.
